title: Agency Substages in AI-Augmented Software Development
summary_markdown: This document enumerates the substages of the Agency axis. Each substage represents a stable configuration of responsibility, authority, and attention between humans and AI systems. Progression is non-linear but ordered; later substages require earlier capacities to be internalized or delegated.
core_assumptions: Agency transfer is the dominant system constraint shaping all other improvement axes.
Software systems increasingly act, decide, and adapt without human intervention.
Humans retain responsibility for grounding: choosing realities, purposes, and values.
All durable knowledge lives in source control; context is ephemeral.

substages.name: Manual Coding
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Human manually writes all code, tests, and designs. No AI involvement.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: Human commands and executes the entire loop.
substages.example_markdown:
A COBOL team at a regional bank writes mainframe code in vim, submits batch jobs to compile, runs test jobs manually, and debugs by reading JCL output and core dumps. Every change requires understanding the full execution path.

Early 2000s Java shops: developers run `javac` manually, execute tests one class at a time, and deploy by copying WAR files to servers via FTP.

emotional_state.discomfort: Everything depends on me; progress is fragile.

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Repetitive manual work; desire for speed or assistance.
readiness.effectiveness_metric_markdown: Cycle time per change; defect escape rate.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of the belief that control requires direct execution.

readiness_to_enter_signals: Pain from slow feedback loops is visible.
Desire to reduce manual cognitive load.
letting_go_to_progress: The belief that understanding everything is required to act.

substages.name: Heavy Tooling
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Humans rely on IDEs, linters, static analysis, and CI to catch errors early.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: Human commands the loop; tools provide guardrails.
substages.example_markdown:
A React team uses VS Code with ESLint, Prettier, and TypeScript. Red squiggles appear instantly for type errors. Every PR triggers GitHub Actions running tests, type checks, and linting. The IDE catches most syntax errors before the developer even saves.

A 2019 startup using JetBrains IDEs with real-time inspections, SonarQube for code quality, and CircleCI pipelines—comprehensive tooling, but no AI assistance.

emotional_state.discomfort: I rely on tools, but I still carry all decisions.

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Tools catch many issues; remaining work is still repetitive.
readiness.effectiveness_metric_markdown: Defects caught pre-merge; CI pass rate.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of the belief that tools are the same as delegation.

readiness_to_enter_signals: Most errors are mechanical rather than conceptual.
effectiveness_measures: Time spent fixing style or syntax issues
letting_go_to_progress: The belief that tools replace thinking.

substages.name: Scripted Automation
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Humans encode repeatable steps into scripts and pipelines.
substages.example_markdown:
A team uses a Makefile with targets like `make build`, `make test`, `make deploy`. Their Jenkins pipeline runs on every merge to main: build → test → staging deploy → smoke tests → production deploy. Known workflows are captured in scripts; novel work is still manual.

Netflix's Spinnaker pipelines circa 2018: fully automated deployment with canary analysis, automated rollback, and zero-touch releases—but every pipeline step was explicitly scripted by humans.

emotional_state.discomfort: Automation helps, but only for what I already understand.

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Scripts work reliably; novelty remains manual.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of the idea that automation must be pre-planned.

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that automation must be deterministic to be useful.

substages.name: Mechanical Leverage
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: AI is used for autocomplete and snippet generation.
substages.example_markdown:
A developer types `function fetchUserById(id: string)` and GitHub Copilot autocompletes the entire function with try/catch, fetch call, error handling, and TypeScript types. They tab through suggestions while building a CRUD API, accepting about 40% of completions.

A developer writes a comment `// parse ISO date string to Date object` and Copilot generates the implementation. Another types `describe('UserService',` and Copilot scaffolds the entire test suite structure.

emotional_state.discomfort: It helps, but I still own everything.

readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of typing as the primary value.

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that writing code equals designing software.

substages.name: Cognitive Mirror
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: AI is used to explain, critique, and question human thinking.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: Human decides; AI reflects and challenges.
substages.example_markdown:
A developer pastes a validation function into ChatGPT and asks 'What edge cases am I missing?' The AI identifies that the code doesn't handle empty strings, unicode whitespace, or null inputs. The developer adds test cases for each and updates the implementation.

An architect asks Claude to critique their database schema design. Claude points out a missing index on a frequently-queried foreign key and suggests denormalizing a junction table that would cause N+1 query problems.

readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of ego around being right.

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that insight must originate from humans.

substages.name: Delegated Execution
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Agents perform bounded tasks under human direction.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human selects and scopes each task
Human reviews output before accepting
Human remains available for clarifying questions
substages.example_markdown:
A developer tells Claude Code: 'Add cursor-based pagination to the /users endpoint. Use a default limit of 20 and encode the cursor as base64.' Claude Code reads the existing code, implements the pagination, updates the tests, and presents the changes for review.

A Cursor user selects a failing test, opens Agent mode, and says 'Fix this test—the API response format changed.' The agent traces the failure, updates the test expectations, and verifies the fix passes.

emotional_state.discomfort: Will it do what I meant?

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that quality requires human execution.

substages.name: Repeatable Delegation
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Delegation is standardized via scripts, templates, and prompts.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: Human defines pattern; AI executes repeatedly.
substages.example_markdown:
A team creates a `/generate-api-endpoint` slash command in Claude Code stored in `.claude/commands/`. New developers type `/generate-api-endpoint users` and get a consistent implementation following team patterns: route, controller, service, repository, and tests—all matching the established architecture.

A Cursor team maintains a `.cursorrules` file that encodes their React patterns, testing conventions, and code style. Every agent interaction inherits these rules, ensuring consistent output across the team without per-prompt instruction.
letting_go_to_progress: The belief that humans must adapt every time.

substages.name: Closed Local Loops
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Agents run until local correctness conditions are met.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human defines correctness conditions (tests, linting, type checks)
Human trusts the loop to iterate without watching each attempt
Human reviews final outcome when conditions are satisfied
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: AI iterates locally; human reviews outcome.
substages.example_markdown:
Claude Code enters a fix loop: attempt a change → run `npm test` → read the failure output → try a different approach. After four iterations, the tests pass. The developer returns to find a green build and a commit ready for review—they never saw the intermediate failures.

Devin works on a bug fix, running the reproduction script after each change. It tries three different approaches, each time checking if the script passes. On success, it runs the full test suite to check for regressions, then commits.

emotional_state.discomfort: What if the tests are wrong?

readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of supervising every attempt.

letting_go_to_progress: The need to watch every loop.

substages.name: Step-Level Process Supervision
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: AI runs the defined process; human observes every step.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human decomposes work into tasks
Human defines how each step should flow
Human fixes the process definition when steps go wrong
Human sees every commit, every task boundary, every small decision
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: AI executes the process; human supervises at step granularity and fixes process definitions.
substages.example_markdown:
A team's CLAUDE.md encodes their process: 'Write failing tests first. Implement to make tests pass. Run linter and type-check before committing. Commit messages must reference issue numbers.' A developer gives Claude Code a task. After each commit, they review the diff—not to check the code, but to verify Claude followed the process. When Claude starts writing implementations before tests, they add to CLAUDE.md: 'You MUST create test files before implementation files. I will reject commits that don't follow this order.'

At Anthropic, engineers maintain team CLAUDE.md files encoding their conventions. When reviewing Claude's commits, an engineer notices commit messages consistently lack context. Rather than editing messages manually, they update CLAUDE.md: 'Commit messages must explain WHY the change was made, not just WHAT changed. Include the problem being solved.' The next commits include context. The engineer's job has shifted from writing code to supervising and refining the encoded process.

emotional_state.discomfort: I see everything, but I'm not doing it anymore.

failure_modes_markdown: Process fixes are too reactive and local
Human becomes a bottleneck for decomposition

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Step-level process is stable; human notices they're rarely intervening at step boundaries.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of step-level visibility; trust loop-level observation.

readiness_to_enter_signals: Process runs smoothly at step level.
Human interventions at step boundaries are rare.
AI decomposition is trustworthy within goals.
failure_modes: Local fixes that don't address systemic issues
letting_go_to_progress: The need to observe every step of the process.

substages.name: Loop-Level Process Supervision
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: AI runs the process; human observes every iteration cycle.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human defines goals for each loop
Human participates in demos and reviews at cycle boundaries
Human improves the process at the iteration level
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: AI executes steps and decomposes within loops; human supervises at iteration boundaries and fixes process cycles.
substages.example_markdown:
A startup's process doc defines their AI sprint structure: 'Monday: PM tags issues as sprint-ready with acceptance criteria. Throughout week: Droids pull from sprint backlog, implement, create PRs. Each PR must include tests and update docs. Friday: Demo completed features to PM.' The PM doesn't watch commits—they attend Friday demos. When demos consistently show features missing error handling, they update the process: 'Acceptance criteria must include error states. Demo must show both happy path and failure modes.'

A team uses Devin in two-week sprints. Their encoded process specifies: PRs over 200 lines need a design doc, API changes need migration notes, each sprint ends with a retrospective. The tech lead reviews PRs at the sprint boundary, not mid-sprint. After several sprints where large PRs cause merge conflicts, they adjust the process: 'PRs must be under 100 lines. Larger changes must be split into stacked PRs.' They're tuning the iteration process itself, not fixing individual PRs.

emotional_state.discomfort: I don't see every step, but I see every cycle complete.

failure_modes_markdown: Goal definition too vague for AI decomposition
Process improvements lag behind cycle feedback

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Loop-level process is stable; human notices cycles are predictable and demos rarely surface surprises.
readiness.effectiveness_metric_markdown: Iteration success rate; goal achievement accuracy.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of loop-level engagement; trust flow-level observation.

readiness_to_enter_signals: Demos rarely require significant course correction.
failure_modes: Over-optimizing individual cycles vs. overall flow
letting_go_to_progress: The need to participate in every iteration cycle.

substages.name: Flow-Level Process Supervision
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: AI runs the process; human observes overall flow and approach.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human provides strategic direction
Human monitors process health
Human tunes the cycle structure itself
Human handles exceptions when they arise
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: AI executes loops and defines iteration goals; human supervises flow, provides direction, and fixes the overall process structure.
substages.example_markdown:
A VP of Engineering manages an AI development org on Factory. The layered process includes: product roadmap (quarterly objectives), sprint templates (iteration structure), and quality gates (merge criteria). Droids run weekly sprints autonomously. The VP reviews a monthly dashboard: velocity trends, feature completion, tech debt metrics. When the dashboard shows code review quality declining, they investigate and find PRs are being rubber-stamped. They adjust the process by adding a rule: 'Changes touching more than 3 files require architecture review before merge.'

A CTO oversees 50 Droids at an AI-native company. The encoded process includes: issue triage rules, sprint cadence, escalation triggers, and OKR tracking. The CTO doesn't attend sprint demos—they review quarterly progress. When Q2 shows 'improve performance' objectives consistently miss while 'add features' exceeds targets, they recognize a process bias. They update the goal-selection criteria: 'Performance objectives get 1.5x weight in sprint planning.' The fix is to the process, not to any individual sprint.

emotional_state.discomfort: I don't see every loop, but I understand the rhythm and can intervene when needed.

failure_modes_markdown: Strategic drift goes unnoticed between check-ins
Process dysfunction visible only in aggregate
Losing touch with what's actually being built

readiness.ready_to_experiment_markdown: Flow is stable; human notices their process interventions are rare and the system self-corrects.
readiness.effectiveness_metric_markdown: Flow health indicators; exception frequency; strategic alignment.
readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of flow supervision; trust escalation-based engagement.

readiness_to_enter_signals: Process runs smoothly without regular human check-ins.
Strategic direction holds across multiple cycles.
failure_modes: Meta-process changes have unexpected cascade effects
letting_go_to_progress: The need to understand and tune the development process.

substages.name: Hard-Coded Escalation
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: System escalates to humans on predefined conditions.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human defines escalation triggers upfront
Human handles escalated exceptions when they arise
Human refines escalation rules based on experience
substages.example_markdown:
Dependabot opens a PR to update lodash from 4.17.20 to 4.17.21. CI runs automatically. Tests pass, so the PR sits awaiting human merge. On another repo, the same update breaks a test—after three failed CI runs, Dependabot sends a Slack alert to the on-call developer.

GitHub Copilot coding agent works on an issue but hits a compile error it can't resolve after 5 attempts. It stops, marks the PR as 'needs human input', and adds a comment explaining where it got stuck. A developer picks it up the next morning.

emotional_state.discomfort: Did we anticipate the right cases?

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that humans must be the first line of defense.

substages.name: Review-Gated Autonomy
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Long autonomous runs with periodic human review.
secondary_behaviors_markdown: Human reviews accumulated work at defined intervals
Human validates direction and quality
Human authorizes continuation or can halt/redirect
substages.example_markdown:
GitHub Copilot coding agent works overnight on five assigned issues. By 9am, there are five draft PRs waiting. The developer spends 30 minutes reviewing diffs, approves three, leaves comments on one, and closes one that misunderstood the issue. The agent resumes work on the commented PR.

Nubank uses Devin for a multi-month migration of their ETL monolith to sub-modules. Engineers review Devin's PRs weekly, validating that migration patterns are correct. They achieved 12x efficiency improvement with human review only at weekly checkpoints, not per-commit.

readiness.let_go_focus_markdown: Let go of needing full context.

letting_go_to_progress: The belief that understanding requires observation.

substages.name: Ralph Wiggum Mode
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Simple loop, back-pressure, and a law that all durable state lives in source control.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: AI runs continuously; humans are consulted only on demand.
substages.example_markdown:
Geoffrey Huntley ran Claude in a `while true` loop for three months with a single prompt: 'Make me a programming language like Go but with Gen Z slang keywords.' The result was CURSED—a complete language with lexer, parser, LLVM compiler backend, interpreter mode, partial standard library, and editor extensions. Keywords include `slay` (func), `sus` (var), `bestie` (for), and `ඞ` for pointers. Total cost: $14,000 across three implementation attempts. The AI worked autonomously; Huntley provided direction but didn't supervise iterations.

At a Y Combinator hackathon, teams ran Ralph loops overnight: define a PRD, start the loop, go to sleep. By morning: 6 complete repositories, working code, passing tests—$297 in API costs for work estimated at $50k in contractor time. The prompt never changed, but the world did: each iteration saw the modified files and git history from previous runs.

emotional_state.discomfort: I don’t know what it’s doing right now.

letting_go_to_progress: The need to know what is happening.

substages.name: Event-Driven Autonomous Systems
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Agents respond to events rather than running a loop; flows are non-deterministic but grounded by source control.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: System reacts; humans are normative anchors.
substages.example_markdown:
A GitHub App ecosystem: when a PR is opened, one agent runs security scans, another generates documentation diffs, a third suggests test coverage improvements, and a fourth checks for API breaking changes. Each agent fires independently on the webhook event. Their outputs appear as separate PR comments within minutes.

When a production alert fires, an agent investigates logs and opens a diagnostic issue. That issue triggers another agent to attempt a fix. If the fix PR passes CI, yet another agent auto-merges it. The human sees: alert → investigation → fix → deploy, all in their Slack timeline.

substages.name: Personalized Autonomous Software
substages.keystone_behavior_markdown: Software is generated and evolved per human reality; no shared product.
substages.agency_allocation_markdown: System serves an individual; human provides grounding.
substages.example_markdown:
In 2035: your personal AI maintains software tailored to your life. Your fitness app knows your injury history and adjusts workouts. Your budgeting tool learned your spending patterns and handles recurring bills. Your home automation anticipates your routines. None of this was downloaded from an app store—it was generated and evolved by your AI.

A small business owner describes their workflow problems to their AI. Over the next week, the AI builds a custom inventory management system, a customer notification tool, and integrations with their existing accounting software—all bespoke, all maintained by the AI, none available to any other business.
letting_go_to_progress: The idea of software as a product.
